name: hvac (HashiCorp Vault Python Client)

auth.notes: Vault token, AppRole, AWS IAM, Kubernetes service account, LDAP, JWT

performance.notes: REST API wrapper; Vault server response time dominates

agent_readiness.notes: Python client for HashiCorp Vault. import hvac. client = hvac.Client(url='http://localhost:8200', token='s.xxx'). client.secrets.kv.v2.read_secret_version(path='myapp/config'). client.secrets.kv.v2.create_or_update_secret(path='myapp/config', secret={'db_password': 'xxx'}). Dynamic secrets: client.secrets.database.generate_credentials('my-role'). AppRole auth: client.auth.approle.login(role_id, secret_id). Transit encryption: client.secrets.transit.encrypt_data(name='key', plaintext=b64). Kubernetes auth for in-cluster.

gotchas: Token TTL management — handle token renewal or use agent for auto-renewal
KV v1 vs v2 have different API paths (client.secrets.kv.v1 vs .v2)
No built-in async support — use sync client in async contexts carefully
best_when: Retrieving secrets from HashiCorp Vault in Python applications
Dynamic database credentials via Vault database secrets engine
Kubernetes workload authentication to Vault via service accounts
avoid_when: When Vault Agent or Vault Secrets Operator handles secret injection
Simple environment variable secrets (overkill without Vault cluster)
Async Python code (no native async support)
summary: hvac is the Python client for HashiCorp Vault. hvac.Client(url, token).secrets.kv.v2.read_secret_version(path='myapp/config') for KV secrets. Dynamic credentials via database secrets engine. Transit encryption. Auth methods: AppRole, Kubernetes service account, AWS IAM, LDAP. KV v1 vs v2 APIs differ. Token TTL management needed. Use Vault Agent for automatic token renewal.
